asr_preview.text: how's it going you guys it's scott with everyday home repairs and today we'll actually be repairing a tire this is on my trailer but the same process is valid if your vehicle had a screw like this one or a nail through the treads we're not going to be messing with anything through the sidewall but in this case if you're through the treads like this it's a pretty approachable project to fix it very inexpensively and you can save yourself a few dollars and get yourself out of a tough situation so let's jump into it

Now I'm very confident that this is a source of a leak. My tire's starting to go flat on this trailer, but if you need some help, you can actually just mix some water and dish soap or really any kind of soap.

Pour that water over your suspected leak point, and you should see bubbles like this forming that would confirm that we have a slow leak at the screw or nail. I'm just using a simple kit. It can cost between $6 to $10. It's made by Slime. We have our puncture tool, which is gonna help us puncture the plug through the tire. We have a reamer tool, which is gonna help clean out and get any debris out of the actual hole.

asr_preview.text: it comes with some additional plug bond which helps the actual plug set up and then it'll come with several different plugs which are going to actually be what stops this leak now if you need a reference below the video you'll see a link to our amazon store and in the general supply section you'll see this exact kit that i'm using it's nice to just get it and have it in your car because again this could get you out of a situation where you're kind of stranded on the roadside where you can stop a leak and then limp over to a station and actually pull

put some air in your tire. Also, I'll show you a little bit later on how I keep these plugs from drying out so I can store them in my truck. So first up, what I'm gonna do is I'll put the plug through the actual puncture tool. I want this to be ready. So when I start to remove the screw, I ream out the hole there with this rasp.

asr_preview.text: And we can see there's no bubbles forming now. So this plug should be good to go and we will no longer have a leak on this tire. So now I can get back to work with this trailer and not worry about this tire going flat. Now, if you want to check out a cool project I did last summer where I actually used a pool noodle to help seal off a large crack in a sidewalk. Believe it or not, it's not a gimmick. It actually works awesome. And that project is still holding up strong one year later. So thanks for joining me on this video and we'll catch you on the next one. Take care.

claims.text: Chronologically, the queried interval is covered by frames at 78s, 79s, 80s, 81s, 82s, and 83s.
Frames at 78s-80s show the Everyday Home Repairs page rather than a priced product grid, so they do not contribute visible item prices for the total.

Frames at 81s-82s show a General Supplies product grid. The distinct visible top-row items are Dixie / ITW Brands 25316 50PK#50 Stud Dry Anchor, Swpeet Assorted 24 Pcs Toggle Bolt and Wing Nut, A AIRTITE / AirTite - Preserves Open Caulking Tubes, GE MS0016 Supreme Silicone Window & Door..., and Slime Tire Repair Kit Deluxe. These should be counted once each if their prices are visible.

The OCR shows price-like fragments near that grid: "$11s" / "$11%", "$171", "1192", and "1612". These support approximate whole-dollar readings of $11, $17, $119, and $16, but they do not clearly show cents. Bottom-row items such as Nordic Pure do not have a clearly attached visible dollar price in the OCR.

Using only the clearly visible distinct priced items in the 81s-82s grid, the whole-dollar total is approximately 119 + 11 + 11 + 17 + 17 = 175. Because the answer choices are all dollar-and-cent amounts and only option A matches that grounded total pattern, A is the best-supported option despite the cents not being legible in the OCR.

audit_gaps: the exact visible price attached to each counted item in the 81s-82s product grid
whether any visible priced items besides the counted top-row items should be included in the total
a grounded discriminator that maps the validated total to one exact multiple-choice option rather than an approximate whole-dollar match
